title: Caramel fudge
subtitle: Condensed milk fudge cooked to the soft-ball stage
excerpt: Butter, sugar, and condensed milk cooked to exactly the soft-ball stage, then beaten vigorously off the heat until the mixture grains and sets. Cuts into clean squares with a crumbly, melt-in-the-mouth texture.
sourceNotes: Condensed milk fudge is a British sweet-making tradition that became widespread in the late nineteenth century once condensed milk was commercially available. Mrs Beeton covers fudge in later editions of Book of Household Management. The Carnation-tin recipe, distributed by Nestlé from the mid-twentieth century, has become the household standard in Britain. The technique of cooking to the soft-ball stage, cooling slightly, then beating to grain follows the classic British approach.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Fudge keeps in an airtight container at room temperature for up to 2 weeks.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: No baking required. Cooked on the hob to 116°C (soft-ball stage).

glossaryTerms.definition: A stage in sugar cooking reached at approximately 112 to 116°C. At this temperature, a small amount of the syrup dropped into cold water can be gathered into a soft, pliable ball that flattens when removed. This is the required temperature for fudge: too low and the fudge will be soft and sticky; too high and it will be grainy and dry.

After cooking, fudge must be cooled to about 60°C and then beaten vigorously until it thickens, loses its gloss, and begins to grain. This beating encourages the formation of many small sugar crystals (rather than a few large ones) and produces the characteristic smooth, crumbly texture. Beating too early (while too hot) produces a runny result; too late and it may seize suddenly in the pan.

content.text: Boiling sugar reaches 116°C. If it contacts skin, run cold water over the area for at least ten minutes and seek medical attention if needed.
Fudge has two critical moments: reaching the correct temperature (
at the right temperature after cooling. A sugar thermometer is not optional; guessing the temperature produces variable results.

Grease and line a 20 cm square tin. Put the sugar, butter, milk, and condensed milk in a deep, heavy saucepan. Stir over low heat until the sugar dissolves. Bring to a rolling boil, then cook at a steady boil, stirring constantly, until the mixture reaches 116°C on a sugar thermometer. This takes approximately 15 to 20 minutes. Keep stirring — condensed milk burns easily on the base.

Remove from heat. Add the vanilla and salt. Allow to cool undisturbed until the temperature drops to around 60°C, about 10 to 15 minutes. Beat vigorously with a wooden spoon — the mixture will thicken, lose its gloss, and become slightly grainy. This takes 5 to 10 minutes of sustained beating. Pour into the lined tin and smooth the surface. Allow to set at room temperature for 2 hours, then cut into squares with a sharp knife.
